coredata.dc:title: Novel dual-emissive fluorescent immunoassay for synchronous monitoring of okadaic acid and saxitoxin in shellfish
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Mingming
Wang, Weijie
Wei, Jie
Chen, Xiaomei
Chen, Quansheng
Chen, Xi
Oyama, Munetaka
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we present a novel dual-emissive fluoroimmunoassay for synchronous monitoring of okadaic acid (OA) and saxitoxin (STX) using multicolor fluorescent labels composed of sulfur, phosphorous co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, P-GQDs), and ovalbumin (OVA)-coated gold nanoparticles (OVA-AuNPs). The novel OVA-AuNPs were prepared by the reduction of chloroauric acid under alkaline conditions using OVA as a reducing agent. Both S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs exhibit bright fluorescence, more importantly, a large emission wavelength difference (Δλ = 156 nm) under an excitation of 400 nm and relatively independent fluorescence behavior, which are essential to realizing the dual-signal marks in a directly mixing system. Using a competitive fluorescence-linked immunosorbent assay (cFLISA) format, the dual-emissive cFLISA was successfully utilized to measure OA and STX contents in Alectryonella plicatula (commonly named as fingerprint oyster) and the detection results were in good agreement with the commercial enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doped graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Okadaic acid
Saxitoxin
Competitive fluorescent immunosorbent assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs
Preparation of coated antigens and fluorescent probes
Simultaneous determination of OA and STX by cFLISA method
Characterization of the S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs
Properties of coated antigens and fluorescent probes
Fluorescence feasibility for synchronous detection of OA and STX
Synchronous detection of OA and STX
The specificity and anti-interference ability of cFLISA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A-B) TEM images of OVA-AuNPs (top inset A: size distribution of OVA-AuNPs; top inset B: HRTEM image of OVA-AuNPs). (C) FTIR spectrums of OVA and OVA-AuNPs.

(A) UV–vis absorption (black line), PL excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) spectra of OVA-AuNPs. The inset displays photographs of the OVA-AuNPs under UV irradiation (left) and daylight (right) in aqueous solution. (B) PL spectra of OVA-AuNPs at different excitation wavelengths. (C) The effect of pH values on the PL intensity of OVA-AuNPs. (D) The effect of NaCl concentration on the PL intensity of OVA-AuNPs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of STX, BSA and STX-BSA. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of OVA-AuNPs, Anti-STX-MAb and OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb. (C)The dynamic light scattering spectra of OVA-AuNPs, Anti-STX-MAb and OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb. (D) PL spectra of the OVA-AuNPs and OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb. (E) PL intensities of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb at different temperatures. (F) PL intensities of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb in different dilutions.

(A and B) Effect of dilution ratio of (A) coated antigen and (B) immunoprobes on the IC50 of the cFLISA for OA and STX detection. (C and D) The calibration curve for respective detection of (C) OA and (D) STX. (E and F) The calibration curve for simultaneous detection of (E) OA and (F) STX.
Illustration of the construction of cFLISA for OA and STX detection.
Results for OA and STX in Alectryonella plicatula by the proposed cFLISA, MS and commercial ELISA kits (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The diluted pretreatment solutions were detected directly.

head.ce:title: Novel dual-emissive fluorescent immunoassay for synchronous monitoring of okadaic acid and saxitoxin in shellfish

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we present a novel dual-emissive fluoroimmunoassay for synchronous monitoring of okadaic acid (OA) and saxitoxin (STX) using multicolor fluorescent labels composed of sulfur, phosphorous co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, P-GQDs), and ovalbumin (OVA)-coated gold nanoparticles (OVA-AuNPs). The novel OVA-AuNPs were prepared by the reduction of chloroauric acid under alkaline conditions using OVA as a reducing agent. Both S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs exhibit bright fluorescence, more importantly, a large emission wavelength difference (Δλ = 156 nm) under an excitation of 400 nm and relatively independent fluorescence behavior, which are essential to realizing the dual-signal marks in a directly mixing system. Using a competitive fluorescence-linked immunosorbent assay (cFLISA) format, the dual-emissive cFLISA was successfully utilized to measure OA and STX contents in Alectryonella plicatula (commonly named as fingerprint oyster) and the detection results were in good agreement with the commercial enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kits.

ce:section.ce:para: Although immunoassays are long-known, generous efforts have been made worldwide with the aim of producing user-friendly devices with high throughput, high sensitivity, good robustness, and low cost. Generally, the immunoassay is implemented based on signal generation after the immune interaction between the antibody and the antigen. Therefore, the signal label is one of the most important factors influencing the performance of immunoassays. As a typical sensing method, the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) has been gaining attention since a rather long time due to its low cost, simplicity, and practicality (Jiang, Rao, Mittl, & Hsieh, 2020; Jia et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). However, the inherent limitations of enzymes and insufficient sensitivity hindered their broad application. In contrast to the traditional ELISA, fluorescence-linked immunosorbent assay (FLISA) uses fluorescent materials as markers, and thus, it exhibits superior performance in terms of lifetime and stability. However, challenges remain relevant in FLISA, especially high throughput and high sensitivity. As far as we know, in most high-throughput FLISAs, organic fluorescent dyes such as rhodamine B and fluorescein isothiocyanate are widely used as multicolor labels (Aydin et al., 2021; Lei et al., 2019; Mazi et al., 2021; Shan et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2018; Thi Tran, Phan, Nguyen, & Ju, 2020). These dyes emit bright fluorescence; however, they possess biological toxicity and should be excited at a specific excitation wavelength, and are easily oxidized and bleached. In recent years, with the rapid development of nanotechnology, various luminescent quantum dots (QDs) have been prepared (Atchudan et al., 2018; Feng & Liu, 2018; Luo et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2019). Unfortunately, only a few of them were applied as multicolor labels in FLISAs. This lack of application may be due to the construction of high-throughput FLISAs. The marks should possess bright fluorescence and large emission wavelength differences under the same excitation wavelength. In addition, their fluorescence behavior should not interfere with each other. Unfortunately, most of the QDs could not meet these requirements in a direct mixing system. Therefore, ongoing efforts are still needed to discover novel immunolabels in high-throughput FLISAs.

Recently, fluorescent gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been widely used in fluorescent biosensors owing to their excellent optical properties and good biocompatibility (Li et al., 2018a; Li et al., 2018b; Liang et al., 2018; Yang, Lu, Sun, Yuan, & Lu, 2018). Moreover, it has been proven that by adjusting the synthesis conditions, these fluorescent AuNPs could be generated at different emission wavelengths, which opens new possibilities for the development of multicolor immunolabels. On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained great attention as fluorescent probes for the determination of various targets (Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019a; Wang et al., 2019b). In our previous works, we found that doping GQDs with multiple heteroatoms, such as S/P (Wang et al., 2019a) and P/Cl (Wang et al., 2019b), can distinctly improve their fluorescent activity and quantum yield (QY). These developments in the multicolor fine-tuning of GQDs and AuNPs motivated us to develop dual-emissive immunomarkers in FLISAs. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report focusing on tuning the fluorescence of GQDs and AuNPs to establish a dual-emissive fluoroimmunoassay.

Shellfish toxins are usually accumulated in shellfish through consumption of toxic algae, responsible for producing the toxins. Various shellfish toxins can easily coexist in the same shellfish owing to the filter-feeding characteristics of shellfish. It is necessary to develop a fast, sensitive, and simultaneous detection method for shellfish toxin to analyze shellfish contamination with high efficiency and immediately put forward a diagnosis and treatment plan for shellfish poisoning. Okadaic acid (OA), a main component of diarrhetic shellfish poisoning (DSP), is a lipid-soluble marine biotoxin mainly produced by dinoflagellates (Fu, Zhao, Ji, & Xu, 2019). Saxitoxin (STX), one of the main toxins of paralytic shellfish poisoning, is one of the most toxic marine toxins (Chen, Jia, Wang, & Hu, 2020). The amounts of both OA equivalents and STX equivalents in shellfish have been strictly monitored. For OA equivalents, the maximum permitted level in seafood is 160 μg kg−1, and for STX equivalents, the amount is 800 μg kg−1 according to European Regulation (EC) No 853/2004 (European Union (EU), 2004).

In the present work, we introduce a novel dual-emissive fluoroimmunoassay for simultaneous monitoring of OA and STX in shellfish. S, P-GQDs with blue emission and ovalbumin-coated AuNPs (OVA-AuNPs) with red emission were used as dual-emissive markers to link to the monoclonal antibody of OA (Anti-OA-MAb) and Anti-STX-MAb, respectively. It is surprising that, in a directly mixing system, both immunoprobes possess not only good biocompatibility but also strong and independent fluorescence properties when the excitation wavelength is set to 400 nm. These advantages are the premise for OA and STX to be detected sensitively and selectively by a competitive FLISA (cFLISA) format. In addition, for OA analogues, the cross-reactivity rates to dinophysistoxin-1 (DTX-1) and DTX-2 were both 50%, for STX analogues, the cross-reactivity rates to gonyautoxin-2/3 (GTX-2/3) and decarbamoyl saxitoxin (dc-STX) were 22% and 30%, respectively. Though the proposed method had non-ignorable cross-reactivity to OA and STX analogues, this did not affect the practicality of the proposed method. This is due to that the analogues of both OA and STX usually coexist in naturally contaminated shellfish and are important in terms of regulatory monitoring. The performance of the as-proposed dual-emissive cFLISA for OA and STX determination was further confirmed by the commercial ELISA kits.

OA, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and OVA were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. STX, anti-OA-MAb, and anti-STX-MAb were obtained from Puhuashi Ltd. (Beijing, China). Sodium phytate, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O, 99.9%) were obtained from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Maltose monohydrate, ethanol, methanol, formaldehyde, Tween-20, rhodamine B, and anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99.0%) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was used throughout the experiment. Unless otherwise specified, all reagents used in the study were of analytical grade. In addition, it should be noted that the working ranges of OA kit and STX kit (AbraxisBioScience, USA) are 0.20–5.00 μg/kg and 0.02–0.40 μg/kg, and the detection limits are 0.10 μg/kg and 0.015 μg/kg, respectively. In addition, the cross-reactivity of the OA kit to DTX-1 and DTX-2 were 50 %, the cross-reactivity of the STX kit to dc-STX, GTX-2/3 and gonyautoxin-5B (GTX-5B) were 29 %, 23 %, 23 % (other STX analogs was less than 2 %).

The as-synthesized S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab-250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet IS50), fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer-LS55, USA), UV–vis absorption spectrometer (Lambda 265), and laser particle size analyzer (Mastersizer 2000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: S, P-GQDs were prepared according to a previously reported hydrothermal method (Wang et al., 2019b) (see more details in SI). The synthesis of OVA-AuNPs is shown in Scheme S1. First, 6 mL of 4.9 mmol/L HAuCl4 and 6 mL of 20 mg/mL OVA were added to 25 mL of a single-necked flask and stirred well at room temperature for 2 min. Then, 180 μL of 3 mol/L NaOH was added to the flask, and the mixture was reacted at 80 °C for 40 min. Note that single-necked flask and all glassware used in the experiment were treated as follows: soaked in aqua regia (the volume ratio of concentrated nitric acid to hydrochloric acid was 1:3) for one day, then rinsed with ultrapure water, and finally dried in an oven at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of coated antigens and fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The coated antigens of STX-BSA and OA-BSA were prepared according to previous reports, with some modifications (Jin et al., 2019; Ramalingam, Chand, Singh, & Singh, 2019). Briefly, STX-BSA was obtained by the conjugation of the guanidine groups of STX and the amino groups of BSA in the presence of formaldehyde. OA-BSA was obtained by coupling the carboxyl groups of OA with the amino groups of BSA. Please see SI for more details of the synthesis.

Fluorescent probes were prepared by connecting the markers (S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs) to the antibodies (Anti-OA-MAb and Anti-STX-MAb) via a classic amidation reaction. For OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb, 1.2 mL of 3 mg/mL OVA-AuNPs and 40 μL of 1 mg/mL EDC were first added to a 10-mL flask, and the mixture was shaken at room temperature in the dark for 30 min. Then, 2 mL of Anti-STX-MAb diluted with 0.1 mol/L PBS was added to the activated OVA-AuNPs, and the mixture was shaken in the dark for 2 h. After that, the mixture was treated by dialysis for 24 h to remove unreacted OVA-AuNPs, and then centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 5 min. Finally, the collected precipitate was resuspended in PBS containing 1% BSA and stored at 4 °C for further use. The preparation steps of S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb were similar to those of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of OA and STX by cFLISA method
ce:section.ce:para: The detection process of OA and STX by the cFLISA method is shown in Scheme 1 . It includes the following steps: 1) Coating: OA-BSA and STX-BSA were diluted 100 and 500 times with 0.1 mol/L carbonate buffer solution (CBS) (pH = 9.6), then 100 μL of the above diluents were added to the 96-well plate, incubated at 37 °C for 2 h, and washed with phosphate-buffered saline with Tween20 (PBST) three times. 2) Blocking: 300 μL of 1.5% OVA solution was added to the above mixture, incubated at 37 °C for 50 min to prevent non-specific binding, and then washed with PBST three times. 3) Competition: A series of 50 µL OA and STX standard solutions were added to the plate, and then 50 μL of S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb, and OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb diluted with 1% BSA in PBS were added. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 1 h and washed with PBST 3 times. 4) Determination: 200 μL of PBS containing 1% BSA was added to the plate, and the fluorescence was measured using a PerkinElmer-LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Alectryonella plicatula contaminated by OA equivalents (about 1.2 mg kg−1) and STX equivalents (about 2.7 mg kg−1) were obtained from Xiamen Center for Disease Control and Prevention and treated according to that reported in the literature (Li et al., 2018b). First, 9 mL of methanol: water (v: v = 80: 20) were mixed with 4.00 g of mussels and swirled for 2 min. The solution was then centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was poured into clean beakers. Then, 36 μL of 2.5 mol/L NaOH solution was added to 2 mL of the extract and then heated at 74 °C for 40 min. After that, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and neutralized by 108 μL of a 2.5 mol/L HCl solution. Finally, the resultant was diluted with PBS containing 1% BSA to a desirable concentration.

Spiked samples were prepared by adding different amounts of OA and STX to 4.00 g of non-contaminated mussels (Alectryonella plicatula) and then treated similarly as the above process. The final concentrations of spiked OA were 4.0, 8.0, 10.0 μg/kg and STX were 4.0, 8.0, 10.0 μg/kg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of S, P-GQDs (blue emission with a quantum yield (QY) of 15.69%, excitation-dependence fluorescence behavior) has been described in detail in our previous report (Wang et al., 2019b). Therefore, in this part, we focused on the characterization of the novel fluorescent markers of OVA-AuNPs. Representative TEM images of OVA-AuNPs are shown in Fig. 1 A and B. The well-dispersed OVA-AuNPs have a uniform spherical shape with an average diameter of 7.68 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). The HRTEM image indicates that the OVA-AuNPs are highly crystalline, and the lattice space is 0.240 nm (inset of Fig. 1B). As shown in Fig. 1C, OVA-AuNPs and OVA have obvious stretching vibration peaks at 3393 cm−1 and 2964 cm−1, indicating that abundant –OH, –NH and CH were generated on their surfaces. The infrared absorption peaks at 1655 cm−1 (Vs. that of CO), 1450 cm−1 (Vs. that of NH), and 1076 cm−1 (Vs. that of CC/CN) indicate that the surface of OVA-AuNPs had abundant amino and carboxyl groups. After comparison with OVA, it can be concluded that these functional groups on OVA-AuNPs were mainly derived from OVA molecules. These results, on the one hand, verify the good dispersion of OVA-AuNPs in water, and on the other hand, demonstrate that OVA-AuNPs can provide abundant linkers for coupling with bioactive molecules.

The surface elemental composition and chemical structure of OVA-AuNPs were investigated by XPS. From Fig. S1A, the spectrum of OVA-AuNPs reveals four typical peaks at 84.3, 284.7, 399.7, and 530.9 eV, corresponding to Au 4f, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The atomic percentages of C, O, N, and Au were 42.35%, 34.40%, 21.15%, and 2.10%, respectively. From the high-resolution C 1 s spectra of OVA-AuNPs, three peaks at 284.6, 286.0, and 288.0 eV (Fig. S1B) were attributed to the CC, CN, and CO groups. The high-resolution N 1 s spectra in Fig. S1C reveals two peaks at 399.4 eV and 400.0 eV, relating to the CNC bond and N–C3 structure (Lakkakula, Divakaran, Thakur, Kumawat, & Srivastava, 2018). These results confirmed that abundant amino, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid groups were produced on the surface of OVA-AuNPs, which is consistent with the FTIR results. The high-resolution Au 4f spectra in Fig. S1D reveal peaks centered at 84.0 eV (Au 4f7/2) and 87.7 eV (Au 4f5/2), indicating the Au (0) metallic state in the OVA-AuNPs.

The optical characteristics of the OVA-AuNPs were studied and the results were concluded in Fig. 2 . The UV–vis absorption spectra of OVA-AuNPs in Fig. 2A show two absorption peaks at 220 nm and 280 nm (black line), which are ascribing to the π → π* transition of the CC bond and n → π* transition of the CO bond, respectively. The OVA-AuNPs solution appeared yellow under daylight but red under a UV lamp (365 nm) (inset of Fig. 2A). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum in Fig. 2A demonstrates that the maximum excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) wavelengths of the OVA-AuNPs are 470 and 667 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2B, the maximum emission peak located at 667 nm remained unchanged when the excitation wavelength increased from 450 to 540 nm, indicating that the PL emission of the OVA-AuNPs was excitation-independent. In addition, as presented in Fig. 2C, OVA-AuNPs exhibited strong and stable PL intensity in the pH range of 3.0–13.0. In our experiment, precipitation appeared when the pH was lower than 3.0, which resulted in a decrease in fluorescence. Additionally, the PL behavior of OVA-AuNPs in different concentrations of NaCl solution (0.1–1.0 mol/L) was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2D, the PL intensity of the OVA-AuNPs remained unchanged even at a high concentration of NaCl (1.0 mol/L), indicating that the OVA-AuNPs maintained good fluorescence at high-salt solutions. With rhodamine B (QY = 89% in ethanol at 495 nm) as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yield of OVA-AuNPs was 15.60% (see more details in SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of coated antigens and fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: To study whether the antigens were successfully coated on BSA and the fluorescent markers were covalently linked to the corresponding antibodies, the optical properties and particle size distribution of the resulting product were investigated. As the synthesis process of STX-BSA was the same as that of OA-BSA, STX-BSA is described in detail in this section. Analogically, OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb was used as an example here; thus, the detailed results and discussions S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb are shown in SI (Fig. S2 and S3).

As presented in Fig. 3 A, STX, BSA, and STX-BSA were analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy. It is obvious that STX displays no UV absorption peak, and BSA reveals the characteristic absorption peak of the protein at 280 nm. Compared with STX, after coupling with BSA, a significant UV absorption peak appeared in the UV–vis spectra of STX-BSA, and the location of the absorption peak was similar to that of BSA, evidencing the successful coating of STX on the BSA surface.

As shown in Fig. 1C, abundant carboxyl groups were generated on the surface of OVA-AuNPs. After activation by EDC/NHS, these carboxyl groups were conjugated with the amino groups of Anti-STX-MAb to produce OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb. The conjugation of OVA-AuNPs and Anti-STX-MAb was confirmed by UV–vis absorption (Fig. 3B) and dynamic light scattering spectra (Fig. 3C). It was found that Anti-STX-MAb showed a characteristic absorption peak deriving from the protein at 280 nm (red line). After combination with OVA-AuNPs, the above characteristic absorption peak disappeared, and the absorption peak derived from OVA-AuNPs at 280 nm was blue-shifted (blue line). These results indicate that the carboxyl groups on OVA-AuNPs were covalently coupled with the amino groups on Anti-STX-MAb under the function of EDC, and the n → π* transition of the CO bond in OVA-AuNPs was affected by the condensation reaction, which caused a blue shift of the absorption peak from OVA-AuNPs at 280 nm. Fig. 3C shows the particle size analysis of OVA-AuNPs before and after the conjugation of Anti-STX-MAb. It is observed that the average particle size of OVA-AuNPs and Anti-STX-MAb are 517.6 nm and 1345.2 nm, respectively. Additionally, the size of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb is 1928.0 nm, which is close to the sum of the particle sizes of OVA-AuNPs and Anti-STX-MAb. This result further proves that OVA-AuNPs were successfully marked on the Anti-STX-MAb. The last question is whether the fluorescence performance of OVA-AuNPs was affected after conjugation with Anti-STX-MAb. Fig. 3D presents the fluorescence spectra of OVA-AuNPs and OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb. The position of the emission peak of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb remained unchanged compared with that of OVA-AuNPs. The fluorescence intensity of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb was slightly lower than that of OVA-AuNPs under the same dilution ratio, which might be due to the minor loss of OVA-AuNPs during the activation and purification process of the coupling reaction. However, the emission of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb is strong enough, which has great potential for use as an immunoprobe in FLISA.

The fluorescence stability of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb and S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb played a vital role in the efficient performance of the detection system. The testing temperature and the type of diluted solution, which may greatly influence the fluorescence stability of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb and S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb, were optimized. The testing temperature was first optimized. Fig. 3E shows that the fluorescence intensity of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb at 37 °C was slightly higher than that at 25 °C, and the signals at 37 °C were more stable; thus, 37 °C was selected as the optimized testing temperature. Furthermore, the type of diluted solution was optimized. OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb was diluted with PBS buffer, PBS buffer containing 0.5% BSA, and PBS buffer containing 1% BSA at the same dilution ratio at 25 °C. It was observed that OVA-AuNPs showed poor fluorescence stability when coupled with antibody in PBS buffer (Fig. 3F). BSA is beneficial for achieving stable fluorescence of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb, and in PBS buffer containing 1% BSA, the immunoprobe showed the best stability as well as the largest fluorescence; thus, PBS buffer containing 1% BSA was chosen as the optimized diluted solution.

Similarly, according to the SI results, it is also proved the successful synthesis of OA-BSA and S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb. In addition, the fluorescence of S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb is also available in the detection system of PBS buffer containing 1% BSA at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence feasibility for synchronous detection of OA and STX
ce:section.ce:para: The above results indicate that S, P-GQDs as blue fluorescent markers and OVA-AuNPs as red fluorescent markers were successfully utilized to label Anti-OA-MAb and Anti-STX-MAb, respectively, suggesting their good fluorescence properties and biocompatibility. For simultaneous detection of shellfish toxins, both probes should have different emission wavelengths under the same excitation wavelength, and their emission should not interfere with each other. In our initial attempts, we used other fluorescent AuNPs, such as BSA-AuNPs and glutathione-coated AuNPs. Unfortunately, neither of them could meet these requirements. By adjusting the excitation condition, we found that when the excitation wavelength was set at 400 nm, the mixture of S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs produced two significant emission peaks located at 511 nm and 667 nm (Fig. S4A, red line). When labeled with corresponding antibodies, the emission intensity of both S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb and OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb slightly decreased, but the position of the two peaks remained unchanged under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm (black line), which provides great potential for the subsequent simultaneous detection of OA and STX. To further verify the feasibility of synchronous detection of OA and STX by dual-emissive fluoroimmunoassay, we investigated the interference of the fluorescence peaks between S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb, and OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb. First, OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb of different concentrations were mixed with S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb at the same concentration, and the fluorescence was measured at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm (Fig. S4B). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb significantly increased with an increase in the concentration of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb. However, for S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb, only small changes in fluorescence intensity were observed, indicating the independent PL behavior of OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb. A similar phenomenon was observed for the S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb. These results confirmed that it is feasible to synchronously detect OA and STX toxins with dual-emission fluorescence generated by S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synchronous detection of OA and STX
ce:section.ce:para: After optimization, 0.1 mol/L CBS (pH 9.6), a fixation time of 2 h, a blocking time of 50 min, and an incubation time of 1 h were selected as the construction conditions of the dual-emissive fluoroimmunoassay (Fig. S5). To further create an effective cFLISA system, the effect of the dilution ratio of OA-BSA and STX-BSA on the sensitivity of cFLISA for OA and STX detection was investigated. OA-BSA and STX-BSA were diluted 100, 500, 1000, and 2000 times, respectively. The optimal dilution ratio of the coated antigen was determined according to the 50% inhibitory concentration (IC50) values of the cFLISA system (Fig. 4 A). It was found that the lowest IC50 value for OA and STX detection appeared when OA-BSA was diluted by 500 times, and STX-BSA was diluted 100 times. Generally, larger amounts of antibody resulted in stronger signal intensity but lower sensitivity; thus, the effect of dilution ratio of the antibody conjugates was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4B, the lowest IC50 for OA and STX detection was achieved when the dilution ratio for immunoprobes was 1:10 and 1:5, respectively. Therefore, the S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb, and OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb were diluted 10 times and 5 times, respectively, before incubation with OA and STX.

Before the simultaneous detection of OA and STX, the proposed cFLISA was applied to detect OA and STX, respectively. Each concentration was analyzed in triplicate under the optimal conditions, and the standard curve was fitted by an S-shaped logical four-parameter equation. Fig. 4C shows the calibration curves for OA detection using the cFLISA method. The curve demonstrates that the IC50 value of the equation is 14.2 ng/mL (14.2 μg/kg), the linear range (LR) is 2.0–98.9 ng/mL (2.0–98.9 μg/kg), and the limit of detection (LOD, corresponding to 85% binding) is 1.3 ng/mL (1.3 μg/kg). Similarly, the calibration curve for STX detection by the cFLISA system shows the IC50 value of the equation is 4.9 ng/mL (4.9 μg/kg) (Fig. 4D), the LR is 1.5–15.9 ng/mL (1.5–15.9 μg/kg), and the LOD is 0.2 ng/mL (0.2 μg/kg). As the concentration in the linear range of STX is far lower than the regulatory limit of 800 μg/kg, a further dilution of the samples may be required to test contaminated shellfish at the regulatory limits. These results demonstrate that the cFLISA system has a wide LR and high sensitivity for detecting OA and STX, respectively.

Following the cFLISA processing steps described in 2.4, the capability of the proposed cFLISA system for simultaneous detection of OA and STX under the optimal conditions was verified. As indicated in Fig. 4E and F, the IC50 values of the calibration curves for OA and STX are 17.9 ng/mL (17.9 μg/kg) and 8.5 ng/mL (8.5 μg/kg), the LRs are 2.5–128.0 ng/mL (2.5–128.0 μg/kg) and 2.5–29.5 ng/mL (2.5–29.5 μg/kg), and the LODs are 1.8 ng/mL (1.8 μg/kg) and 0.6 ng/mL (0.6 μg/kg), respectively. In addition, the cFLISA showed good reproducibility, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.26% for OA (10.0 μg/kg, n = 8) and 4.76% for STX (10 μg/kg, n = 8). Compared with the monochromatic fluorescence system for detecting single shellfish toxin, the dual-emissive cFLISA showed almost the same performance (LR and LOD) for simultaneous detection of OA and STX. This result indicates the application of dual-color fluorescence of S, P-GQDs and OVA-AuNPs can realize synchronous detection of shellfish toxins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The specificity and anti-interference ability of cFLISA
ce:section.ce:para: DTX-1, DTX-2, 13-desmethyl spirolide C (SPX-1), GTX-2/3, dc-STX, OA and STX (at concentrations of 5 ng mL−1 for each toxin) were examined to elucidate the specificity and anti-interference ability of the dual-detection system. As shown in Fig. S6, on one hand, OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb revealed no cross-reaction with OA, DTX-1, DTX-2 and SPX-1, which means that OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb can selectively detect STX among these toxins. On the other hand, because GTX-2/3 and dc-STX were belonged to STX equivalents, OVA-AuNPs-Anti-STX-MAb revealed cross-reactivity rates with 22% and 30% for GTX-2/3 and dc-STX, respectively, indicating that the proposed method is ideal for STX-group toxins monitoring. Similarly, because of the similar structures of OA, DTX-1, and DTX-2, which all belong to the DSP toxin family, S, P-GQDs-Anti-OA-MAb had a relatively high cross-reactivity rates (50%) with DTX-1 and DTX-2, which means that the proposed cFLISA is capable of monitoring OA-group toxins in seafood (Stewart, Elliott, Walker, Curran, & Connolly, 2009). Moreover, SPX-1, GTX-2/3, dc-STX and STX had no cross-reaction with OVA-AuNPs-Anti-OA-MAb. These results demonstrate that our proposed cFLISA method has good specificity and anti-interference ability for the detection of OA equivalents and STX equivalents.

The proposed system was applied to detect OA and STX in contaminated Alectryonella plicatula to verify the feasibility of the cFLISA system for practical application. As indicated in Table 1 , the detected values of OA and STX agreed well with the results obtained by the mass spectrometry (MS) and commercial ELISA kits, suggesting the good applicability of the as-proposed dual-emissive cFLISA in real samples. In addition, the recovery results were comparable with that obtained from ELISA kits (Table S1), suggesting a high level of accuracy of the proposed cFLISA. Additionally, the RSDs of the detection by cFLISA were below 3.0, more favorable than those by ELISAs, further indicating the superiority of the developed cFLISA. These results demonstrate the advantages of the proposed cFLISA system for the simultaneous detection of two types of shellfish toxins in real samples and practical applications.

This work developed a novel dual-emission cFLISA based on OVA-AuNPs and S, P-GQDs for the synchronous detection of OA and STX with high sensitivity and selectivity. OVA-AuNPs emitting red fluorescence applied as the fluorescent marker of Anti-STX-MAb for STX detection; S, P-GQDs emitting blue fluorescence applied as the fluorescent marker of Anti-OA-MAb for OA detection. Their outstanding fluorescence behavior, including strong and independent fluorescence emission and the large emission wavelength difference at a fixed excitation wavelength, ensured the feasibility of the dual-emissive cFLISA. As systematically explored by experiments, our proposed cFLISA system can realize synchronous detection of both OA and STX in a directly mixing system. Though the interference test reveals that the cross-reactivity to OA and its analogues, as well as STX and its analogues is non-ignorable, the proposed cFLISA still has good practicality due to the fact that the analogues of both OA and STX usually coexist in naturally contaminated shellfish and are important in terms of regulatory monitoring. In addition, the cFLISA system was successfully applied to simultaneously detect OA and STX in contaminated Alectryonella plicatula, confirming its practicality. This work opens up new opportunities for exploring dual-emissive fluoroimmunoassays and reveals their potential for use in the multiplex detection of trace amounts of toxins in food safety.

Mingming Yin: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing – original draft. Weijie Wang: Data curation, Investigation, Software. Jie Wei: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiaomei Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Quansheng Chen: Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Xi Chen: Validation. Munetaka Oyama: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32072299, 21675062), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Fujian Province, China (2020 J01677), and the Youth Innovation Foundation of Xiamen (3502Z20206023). We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.cn) for English language editing.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.130856.
